reasoning:
The baseline value is defined as the 10-year Treasury yield on 2026-04-12. Because the FRED series WGS10YR reports weekly averages ending on Friday, this likely corresponds to the week ending April 10, 2026. Daily Treasury yields leading up to April 10 averaged around 4.30%, but surged on Friday, April 10 to close at 4.34%. 

1. Near Term (April & May 2026): Because the previous week closed at 4.34% but averaged around 4.31%, the next week starts with a mechanical head-start of roughly 3 basis points above the previous week's average. Against a typical 8 bps weekly standard deviation, this significantly shifts the distribution for April 19 and May 12 upwards via momentum and starting position, yielding estimates above 0.5 (0.62 and 0.56 respectively).
2. Medium Term (July to October 2026): Institutional consensus (such as the Philadelphia Fed SPF and bank surveys) projects yields will gently drift downward to around 4.25% by the end of 2026 as the Fed potentially cuts rates. This expected downward drift offsets the near-term momentum, pushing probabilities slightly below 50% by Fall 2026.
3. Long Term (2027 to 2036): Long-term consensus and historically anchored forward rates typically settle around 3.9%–4.1%, which is below the ~4.31% baseline. This structural regression to a lower mean implies probabilities below 0.50 for these later dates. However, as the forecast horizon extends into 3, 5, and 10 years, uncertainty grows substantially, and factors like structural inflation and debt loads could introduce upside risks. Therefore, the probabilities pull closer to 0.5 as time horizons expand, reflecting both the increasing variance and the required adherence to greater uncertainty over time.
